position: For unidirectional server-to-client notification workloads, SSE is the correct architectural default. WebSocket should be chosen only when bidirectional communication is genuinely required — which is far less often than teams assume.
reasoning: SSE rides on HTTP, which means every intermediary (load balancers, CDNs, reverse proxies, corporate firewalls) already knows how to handle it. WebSocket upgrades require explicit configuration at every hop, and misconfiguration at any layer causes silent connection drops that are maddening to diagnose — I've been the one at 3AM chasing a WebSocket that dies at exactly 60 seconds because the AWS ALB idle timeout wasn't documented.

When an SSE connection fails, the client gets a standard HTTP status code. When a WebSocket drops, you get a close frame with a code, or nothing at all — and browser behavior around reconnection varies. SSE's error visibility alone has saved me hours that would have been spent guessing why a WebSocket silently died behind a corporate proxy that blocks upgrade requests.

SSE has built-in reconnection (EventSource) with Last-Event-ID for exactly-once resumption. WebSocket has no standard reconnection — every team implements their own, and most get exponential backoff wrong, creating reconnect storms at scale. I've seen a 50K-connection WebSocket fleet collapse because the homegrown reconnection logic had a bug in the jitter calculation.

For notification payloads — order fills, price updates, status changes — the data flow is fundamentally server-to-client. SSE's unidirectional model maps directly to this, with no overhead for a return channel that goes unused. WebSocket's bidirectional framing means every message carries overhead for a channel you're not using.

SSE native browser support is now excellent (Edge joined the party, Safari works). The polyfill story for older browsers is straightforward. The 'but IE11' argument is basically dead for greenfield applications in 2025.

SSE handles concurrency naturally through HTTP/2 multiplexing — multiple EventSource connections share a single TCP connection without head-of-line blocking. WebSocket over HTTP/2 is still not uniformly supported across CDNs and proxies.

WebSocket's complexity tax is real at scale: you need ping/pong keepalives, connection health monitoring, reconnection state machines, and usually a fallback transport anyway. SSE gives you most of the reliability for free from the HTTP stack.
concerns: SSE has a hard limit of 6 concurrent connections per origin in HTTP/1.1 (browser enforcement). This matters if you need multiple notification streams — though HTTP/2 eliminates this, and in practice most apps need one stream.
SSE doesn't support binary frames natively. If your notifications require binary payloads (protobuf, MessagePack), you need base64 encoding, which adds ~33% overhead.
SSE connections are unidirectional from server, so if the server needs to react to client feedback, you still need a separate out-of-band channel (REST calls, a secondary connection).

Not all reverse proxies handle long-lived SSE connections gracefully — some buffer responses aggressively. This is a configuration issue, not a protocol limitation, but it's a real operational friction point.

If you genuinely have bidirectional messaging — chat, collaborative editing, live cursor positions — then WebSocket's bidirectional framing is the right tool, and SSE's single-direction constraint becomes an awkward fit requiring workarounds.
evidence_needed: Actual traffic profile breakdowns from production notification systems — what percentage of payloads are actually server-to-client vs bidirectional?
Empirical data on SSE vs WebSocket reconnect storm behavior at 50K+ concurrent connections under real proxy configurations (AWS ALB, nginx, Cloudflare, corporate proxies)
Browser SSE connection limit behavior under HTTP/2 multiplexing — does the 6-per-origin limit actually apply in practice with H2?
CDN support matrix for SSE vs WebSocket across major providers — which CDNs support streaming HTTP responses vs requiring WebSocket-specific infrastructure?
Production incident reports where opaque WebSocket failures caused longer MTTR than equivalent SSE failures
